title: Parallel Build Resource Contention
dataSources: Active executor count, node CPU utilization, overlapping job IDs
spl:
index=cicd sourcetype="jenkins:metrics"
| eval utilization_pct=round(busy_executors/total_executors*100,1)
| join type=left max=1 _time [search index=infra sourcetype="kube:node" | stats avg(cpu_usage) as node_cpu by _time]
| where utilization_pct>90 AND node_cpu>85
| table _time, computer, utilization_pct, node_cpu
description: Runner saturation lengthens CI pipelines and causes flaky tests that block releases. Correlating saturation metrics with P95 job duration justifies autoscaling investment and prevents false 'code regression' blame when infrastructure is the root cause.
value: Runner saturation lengthens CI pipelines and causes flaky tests that block releases. Correlating saturation metrics with P95 job duration justifies autoscaling investment and prevents false 'code regression' blame when infrastructure is the root cause.
grandmaExplanation: Runner saturation lengthens CI pipelines and causes flaky tests that block releases — so code changes that fail, leak secrets, or skip checks are caught early.
implementation: Align timestamps between CI and Kubernetes. Alert when high utilization coincides with p95 build time spike. Scale runner pool.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Jenkins metrics, Kubernetes node metrics.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Active executor count, node CPU utilization, overlapping job IDs.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Align timestamps between CI and Kubernetes. Alert when high utilization coincides with p95 build time spike. Scale runner pool.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=cicd sourcetype="jenkins:metrics"
| eval utilization_pct=round(busy_executors/total_executors*100,1)
| join type=left max=1 _time [search index=infra sourcetype="kube:node" | stats avg(cpu_usage) as node_cpu by _time]
| where utilization_pct>90 AND node_cpu>85
| table _time, computer, utilization_pct, node_cpu
```

Understanding this SPL

**Parallel Build Resource Contention** — Runner saturation lengthens CI pipelines and causes flaky tests that block releases. Correlating saturation metrics with P95 job duration justifies autoscaling investment and prevents false 'code regression' blame when infrastructure is the root cause.

Documented **Data sources**: Active executor count, node CPU utilization, overlapping job IDs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Jenkins metrics, Kubernetes node metrics. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: cicd; **sourcetype**: jenkins:metrics. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=cicd, sourcetype="jenkins:metrics". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **utilization_pct** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• Filters the current rows with `where utilization_pct>90 AND node_cpu>85` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Parallel Build Resource Contention**): table _time, computer, utilization_pct, node_cpu


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (utilization vs build time), Table (contention windows), Bar chart (by node pool).
visualization: Line chart (utilization vs build time), Table (contention windows), Bar chart (by node pool).
